translation: When the sky has split [open]
commentary:
إِذَا ٱلسَّمَآءُ ٱنشَقَّتْ

When the sky splits open. This is the 84th surah of the Quran and the final one about which the Prophet ﷺ said that whoever wants to visualize the Day of Resurrection should recite these surahs. Two central themes run through this surah: inevitability and the process by which all of Allah's ﷻ creation reaches its destined conclusion.

This surah builds directly on the themes established in the previous surahs, particularly Surah At-Takwir and Surah Al-Infitar. In those earlier passages, Allah ﷻ described cosmic upheavals – the sun being wrapped up, the sky tearing open – followed by clear consequences: people would know what they brought forward of deeds. By the time we reach Surah Al-Inshiqaq, the "then what happens" portion is left unstated. You are expected to understand it yourself. The warning has been delivered repeatedly; now you should grasp its meaning on your own.

The word انشقت carries profound significance. In Arabic, this term is specifically used for hard physical objects that crack open – rocks splitting apart, mountains fracturing, the earth breaking open. Unlike words that describe tearing soft materials like cloth (which can be stitched back together), انشقت implies something irreversible. When a rock cracks open, there is no putting it back together. This word choice signals finality – this is the end game.

Throughout the Quran, various terms describe what happens to the sky: فُطِرَت ,انفَطَرَت, فُتِحَت. But انشقت is uniquely irreversible, perfectly suited to describe this final moment when everything comes to its ultimate conclusion.

translation: And has responded to its Lord and was obligated [to do so]
commentary:
وَأَذِنَتْ لِرَبِّهَا وَحُقَّتْ

The verb أذنت carries dual meanings in Arabic: to listen carefully and to receive permission. This ayah suggests the sky has been desperately waiting, paying close attention, eager to hear Allah's ﷻ permission to split open. Imagine a student who has been asking repeatedly for an assignment, waiting eagerly for the teacher's permission to begin. When the instruction finally comes, they listen intently and act immediately because they've been waiting for this moment.

This reveals something profound about the nature of creation: the sky actually wants to tear open. It has been held back only because Allah ﷻ has not yet given permission. Nature as we know it is like a restrained beast, desperate to break free but unable to do so until its Master gives the word. The skies don't want to stay intact – they are being held together by Allah's ﷻ command alone.

Why this desperation? The heavens are witnesses to everything that happens on earth. When they observe humanity's disobedience, their sins and arrogance, they react. In another ayah, when someone claims Allah ﷻ has taken a son, the Quran describes how the heavens are about to tear open from such a blasphemous statement. All of Allah's ﷻ obedient creation declares His ﷻ perfection and cannot tolerate disobedience. The sky watches our evil deeds and sins, and it wants to react – but it is held back, waiting for permission.

The phrase وحُقَّت means "as it should be" or "rightfully so." The sky was designed with this ultimate purpose in mind. We typically think of products as being better when they last longer, but some things are designed by their very nature to come to an end. The sky's destruction is actually when it fulfills its complete purpose. It was always meant to do this. The sky will have fulfilled its right, its obligation, when it finally tears open on that Day.

The passive form انشقت is also significant. It doesn't mean something external forced the sky to split; rather, it split on its own – doing what it was designed to do all along. The external force – Allah ﷻ – was actually what held it together. Without that divine restraint, it would have fallen apart long ago.

translation: And when the earth has been extended
commentary:
وَإِذَا ٱلْأَرْضُ مُدَّتْ

When the earth is stretched out. The word مُدَّت describes a process of stretching and flattening. Picture a crumpled sheet or piece of leather with highs and lows, valleys and peaks. When someone pulls and stretches it taut, all those variations become flat. This is what happens to the earth – all the mountains above, all the graves below, everything is stretched into a flat plain.

The idea of stretching implies power and control. Someone must have the ability to manipulate the material to stretch it. This is in the passive form, meaning the earth isn't doing this on its own – Allah ﷻ is stretching it, pulling the earth open and transforming it into a level expanse.

translation: And has cast out that within it and relinquished [it]
commentary:
وَأَلْقَتْ مَا فِيهَا وَتَخَلَّتْ

Here the ayah shifts to active voice – the earth itself throws out whatever has been inside it. The verb ألقت means to throw out forcefully. For ages, the earth has held treasures that people spent their lives seeking. It has held the bodies of the righteous and the sinful alike, buried beneath its surface. Now it can no longer bear their burden. Everything must come out. The secrets of the earth, entire nations buried thousands of years ago that we walk over today without knowing they exist – all of it emerges.

The word تخلّت means to be completely emptied, relieved of a burden, evacuated of contents. The root خلا relates to emptiness, like the empty cells in a honeycomb. When تخلّت is used for a woman, it describes giving birth and finally being relieved of carrying the child. The earth has been pregnant with all these people, holding them inside, and now it cannot take it anymore.

Notice the progression: the earth is stretched (passive – Allah ﷻ does this), and then it throws out its contents (active – the earth does this itself). Allah ﷻ stretches the earth, and as He does, just as a mother's body stretches to allow the child to emerge, the earth throws out everyone it has been carrying. It is finally relieved.

translation: And has responded to its Lord and was obligated [to do so]
commentary:
وَأَذِنَتْ لِرَبِّهَا وَحُقَّتْ

The earth, like the sky, has been seeking permission from its Master. It too has been a witness to the sins of humanity. Every step they took, every word they uttered, every act of disobedience happened on the surface of this earth. The earth itself witnessed Allah's ﷻ disobedience and could not tolerate it, but Allah ﷻ commanded it to remain calm. 

The Quran tells us: "Corruption has appeared on land and sea because of what people's hands have earned" (30:41). The earth reacts to human sin. Sometimes Allah ﷻ allows it to release a little of that reaction, but if the earth reacted to all of humanity's sins at once, the Day of Judgment would begin immediately. On that Day, He will let it fully respond, which is why it will stretch and refuse to hold anyone inside again.

Now we come to a critical point in the surah. When all of this happens – the sky splitting, the earth stretching and emptying itself – there is no "then" statement. In the previous surahs, such descriptions were followed by clear consequences. Here, you should understand what comes next on your own. The warnings have been given repeatedly; by now, you should know.

translation: O mankind, indeed you are laboring toward your Lord with [great] exertion and will meet it
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ إِنَّكَ كَادِحٌ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ كَدْحًا فَمُلَـٰقِيهِ

O humanity, you are marching, struggling, toiling toward your Master, and you will certainly meet Him. The word كادح means to struggle upward, to toil with difficulty, like climbing uphill where each step becomes harder. كَدحًا – this masdar form emphasizes continuous, unrelenting effort. You are marching toward Allah ﷻ every single moment, without respite, whether you acknowledge it or not.

Whether you are inside the masjid trying to remember Allah ﷻ or outside engaged in sin, it makes no difference – you are all headed toward Allah ﷻ, and not one of you is slowing down or speeding up. Everyone is moving at the pace Allah ﷻ has decreed. We are all on a conveyor belt moving toward Him, whether we realize it or not, whether we accept it or not, whether we like it or not.

فملاقيه– the word ملاقاة is different from لقاء. While liqa' means simply to meet, mulaqah means to meet face to face, to stand directly before someone. But here's the remarkable aspect: the grammatical structure treats this future meeting as though it has already occurred. When you say "he will hit the boy," that's future tense. But when you use the construction found here, it's as if the boy has already been hit – it's as good as done. 

This is why the ayah says فملاقيه – you are as good as having already met Him. Consider the meeting done. Even though you are making progress slowly through the stages of life, imagine the journey is complete and you are already there standing before Allah ﷻ.

translation: Then as for he who is given his record in his right hand
commentary:
فَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِىَ كِتَـٰبَهُۥ بِيَمِينِهِۦ

This ayah takes us directly to the end of that journey, skipping all the intermediate stages. Other places in the Quran describe the stages of human development – from a drop of fluid to development in the womb, from childhood to old age, from life to death to the grave to resurrection. Here, because this surah is about finality and conclusion, Allah ﷻ takes us immediately to the final destination.

As for the one whose book is given to him in his right hand. Notice the passive construction: أُوتِىَ – "has been given." The book is no longer in your control. Right now, your book is up to you. Then, it will be handed to you, and whatever is written in it will already be written. We saw in previous surahs how this book is constantly being documented. Now it is being handed over.

The word يمين carries multiple layers of meaning. Literally, it means the right hand, and the people of Paradise are called أصحاب اليمين – the people of the right hand, as opposed to أصحاب الشمال – the people of the left hand.

But yamin in Arabic also means fulfilling a promise. The right hand is used across cultures, including Arabic culture, to seal promises and oaths. These are people who kept their covenant with Allah ﷻ. It is only fitting that they should be honored by receiving the book in their right hand, because the right hand represents the oath fulfilled, the promise kept.

Yamin also represents power, control and freedom. "What your right hand possesses" (ما ملكت أيمانكم) refers to what is under your control and disposal. On Judgment Day, nobody has control over anything. Nobody is speaking freely. Everyone is worried only about themselves. But this person, when he receives his book in his right hand, will have the freedom and empowerment to call out, "Come! Look at my book!" (هَاؤُمُ اقْرَءُوا كِتَابِيَهْ) as we learn elsewhere in the Quran. Who will have such freedom on that Day when everyone else is paralyzed with fear?

Finally, the right hand represents honor and dignity. These are people Allah ﷻ has dignified and honored.

The Prophet ﷺ taught us: "Whoever loves to meet Allah, Allah loves to meet them. And whoever dislikes meeting Allah, Allah dislikes meeting them" (Sahih Bukhari). Aisha (RA) questioned this, saying, "We all dislike death – does this mean we dislike meeting Allah?" The Prophet ﷺ clarified: "It's not like that. When a believer receives the good news of Allah's ﷻ mercy and His pleasure and Paradise, he looks forward to meeting Allah, and Allah loves to meet him. But when the disbeliever hears that punishment and retribution are coming, he hates that idea, and so Allah hates to meet him as well."

translation: He will be judged with an easy account
commentary:
فَسَوْفَ يُحَاسَبُ حِسَابًا يَسِيرًا

He will receive an easy reckoning. Before explaining what this means, imagine the scene: You've received your book in your right hand, but that doesn't mean you're finished. The book must still be checked. The angels are standing there, and you approach them showing your book. "This is my book – I got it in the right hand. Did you notice it's the right hand? Please, on page 75 there's a major good deed. If you could check page 75... and if you look at page 33, there's this significant charity... forgive the mistake on page 47, but that particular prayer had excellent khushu'..."

You're trying to highlight the extra credit assignments, hoping they'll skip over the not-so-good parts, the prayers where you were yawning, the moments when your focus wandered. Having to present your record for evaluation is nerve-wracking in itself.

Let me share a personal experience to illustrate this. After September 11th, 2001, Muslims received "special treatment" at every airport. I once went through TSA at JFK – not a fun experience at the best of times. I went through security, expecting the usual procedure: "Please step aside, sir, we have a special process for you." But I happened to go through around 3 PM when the shifts change. The officer was exhausted. I walked through the detector, and he just glanced at me and gestured – go ahead. I was shocked. "Are you sure?" I wanted to ask. He gave me permission to proceed without questioning, and I was stunned by the ease of it.

حساب literally means audit – counting everything line by line. Aisha (RA) asked the Prophet ﷺ about this "easy reckoning." He explained: "There is not a single person on Judgment Day who is thoroughly questioned except that they will be destroyed. Whoever is questioned in detail on Judgment Day is finished."

She said, "But Allah ﷻ said they would receive an easy reckoning – what about that?" The Prophet ﷺ clarified: "That just means a ceremonial presentation – showing the book and being allowed to proceed. It's not a detailed interrogation."

Think of it like this: you've worked hard on an essay, carefully crafting every word. Some teachers barely look at it – they just check the thickness of the paper, make sure there are actual words and not just drawings, and give you full marks. The idea here is that you have this complete record of all your deeds, but Allah ﷻ has instructed the angels: when their record comes, just look at it ceremonially and let them proceed. No detailed questions. No interrogation.

Allah ﷻ is like the easy grader – the teacher who sees you tried your best and gives full credit. Once they get through to the other side, they feel on top of the world.

translation: And return to his people in happiness
commentary:
وَيَنقَلِبُ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِۦ مَسْرُورًا

He returns to his family full of joy. The scene before was nervously presenting the book, worried and anxious. Now, having passed through successfully, he feels incredible happiness. The word مسرورًا is beautifully chosen. مسرور comes from سرور, which is different from فرح – another word for joy. سرور is happiness that comes from deep within, like a secret sealed inside you. It's the root of سر meaning secret. This is deeply felt happiness that gives genuine comfort and relaxation, like سرير – a place of rest.

This isn't superficial happiness, like laughing at a comedy for a few seconds. This is the kind of profound happiness that moves you to tears – the happiness of reunion with your family.

This raises questions: what about people like Nuh (AS) whose family won't be in Paradise? His wife and son won't be there. How can the ayah say everyone with this easy reckoning returns to their family?

Allah ﷻ teaches us that family – bonds thicker than blood – are the people who believed with you. For some people, their biological family was their torture in this life, their source of pain. Allah ﷻ will replace them with a much better family. They will feel no loss of love, no loneliness. They will have a complete family to return to.

This ayah also addresses a question many women ask: will they even have a family in Paradise? The answer is yes – ينقلب إلى أهله applies to everyone, men and women. All will have family.

But another question arises: the previous surah showed us that even the drinks of heaven vary in quality. Some have drops of the special drink mixed in, while others drink directly from the fountain by Allah's ﷻ company. This means there are different levels in Paradise. What if some family members are at much higher levels than others? The distance between one level of Paradise and the next is like the distance from earth to the stars. How will families reunite?

Allah ﷻ addresses this beautifully: "Those who believed and whose descendants followed them in faith – We will join with them their descendants" (52:21). You won't just meet the family you lived with in this world. You'll meet your great-great-great-grandfather who was the first in your lineage to accept Islam. You'll meet your grandmother who died before you were born, your uncle you never knew. It's a reunion beyond any reunion we can imagine.

Then Allah ﷻ adds: "And We will not deprive them of anything of their deeds" (52:21). If the people at the highest level want to visit family at lower levels, they shouldn't have to downgrade – that would waste their earned rewards. Allah ﷻ promises He will unite them without taking anything away from their higher status. The only possibility remaining is that those at lower levels receive an upgrade to meet their family at higher stations.

This teaches us something profound: if there are people in our family doing exceptional good work – involved in humanitarian efforts, teaching, helping others – instead of ridiculing them ("Why are you doing social work? You could make so much more money!"), we should support them. They might be our upgrade package. They might be the reason we end up at higher levels in Paradise.

So identify such people in your family and encourage them.

مسرورًا – this deeply felt happiness and comfort when reuniting with family. Sometimes even believing family members didn't get along in this world. Both were believers, but they had conflicts. The family reunion at Eid can sometimes turn into a family explosion – the worst comes out when certain relatives gather. So you might worry: there's going to be a giant family reunion in Paradise – is that optional? Can I skip it?

Allah ﷻ removes this concern: "And We will remove whatever is in their hearts of resentment" (15:47). There will be no ill feelings remaining at all. You'll see that uncle and you won't have convulsions. You'll see that cousin and you won't remember what he said. It will all be gone. Even their voice that used to annoy you will sound sweet.

On the other hand, there are mothers who cry every night for the faith of their son or daughter, begging Allah ﷻ to protect them. They worry so much about their children going through trials in college, facing so many temptations. And finally, when that child also makes it to Paradise, they say: "Indeed, we used to fear for them" – they used to be really terrified about their families. The fact that they say "we used to worry" means they no longer worry. Their family is with them now.

They continue: "So Allah conferred favor upon us and protected us from the punishment of the Scorching Fire" (52:27). Allah ﷻ did them a huge favor by saving and protecting their families, allowing them this reunion on Judgment Day. That itself is a massive blessing from Allah ﷻ. May Allah ﷻ grant all of us this favor.

translation: But as for he who is given his record behind his back
commentary:
وَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِىَ كِتَـٰبَهُۥ وَرَآءَ ظَهْرِهِۦ

As for the one who is given his book behind his back. To understand this imagery, we need to picture the scene of criminals on Judgment Day. The Quran describes them as الذين أجرموا – those who committed crimes – they are chained. They march toward Hellfire as prisoners, with their right hand chained to their neck and their left hand chained behind their back. They cannot move either arm.

In another place, the Quran says such a person receives the book in the left hand. But here, an additional detail is provided: وراء ظهره – behind his back. This means not only is he receiving it in his left hand, but he's receiving it where he cannot even see it. He cannot open it to check how bad it is. He's completely blind to his own record.

People behind him can see it and react – "Oh my God, look at what's written!" – but he himself doesn't know how bad it is. He's barricaded even from seeing himself. The previous surah mentioned: "Indeed, from their Lord, that Day, they will be partitioned" (83:15) – they are blocked from seeing their Lord. Now they are also blocked from seeing their own state.

This is the ultimate manifestation of نَسُوا اللَّهَ فَنَسِيَهُمْ – "They forgot Allah, so He made them forget themselves" (9:67). You cannot know your true state when you've forgotten your Master. If you truly know your Master, you know yourself. If you don't know your Master, you don't know yourself. People who forgot their Master won't even get to know where they stand on that Day.

translation: He will cry out for destruction
commentary:
فَسَوْفَ يَدْعُوا۟ ثُبُورًا

He will cry out for destruction. The word ثبور in Arabic means a few things. One meaning is prison – a place of confinement you cannot escape from. Another is something inescapable, unavoidable. The word also carries connotations of death – specifically, a prison sentence that leads to death.

As soon as this person sees that prison (Hellfire), he begs Allah ﷻ: "Please, just give me the death penalty right now. Don't let me go in there." But he will learn what Allah ﷻ tells us in a later surah: لَا يَمُوتُ فِيهَا وَلَا يَحْيَىٰ – "He will neither die therein nor live" (87:13). You don't get to die. You don't get to live either.

Before he's even taken into the Fire, he's already pleading: "Just kill me." In another place, Allah ﷻ says: "Do not call for one destruction today, but call for much destruction" (25:14). When the actual torture begins, they will beg for death again and again and again. When someone is about to be tortured in Hellfire – may Allah ﷻ protect us all – the call will not be for mercy. The call will be for death. Death seems better than what they're experiencing. فسوف يدعوا ثبورًا.

translation: And [enter to] burn in a Blaze
commentary:
وَيَصْلَىٰ سَعِيرًا

He will burn in a Blaze. Allah ﷻ hasn't even taken him inside yet – he's asking for death before that happens. Then he will burn in سعير. Throughout the previous surahs, every time Allah ﷻ described Hell, He used the word جحيم. Now, for the first time in this series, He describes it as سعير.

The word سعير comes from سعر – price or value – and from سعّر – to evaluate the price of something. We evaluate gold, diamonds, the quality of coal by burning a piece to test its combustibility. When Hellfire is called سعير, it means on Judgment Day, Allah ﷻ will put it to the test: How well can it burn?

There's already fire, but now that fire is being pushed to its maximum capacity – like testing a car at full throttle: What's the top speed? What's the acceleration? How far can it go? When Allah ﷻ calls Hellfire سعير, it's the fire that's been tested to its maximum potential.

Some scholars extrapolate from this that سعير means it will never burn out, because wood burns, coal burns, paper burns, but nothing burns like the fuel it was designed for. Allah ﷻ says: "Its fuel is people and stones" (2:24) – stones because they worshipped stones. The human being is the ultimate fuel that pushes Hellfire to its complete test, its full capacity. May Allah ﷻ protect us all.

translation: Indeed, he had [once] been among his people in happiness
commentary:
إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ فِىٓ أَهْلِهِۦ مَسْرُورًا

He used to be joyful among his family. The same word مسرورًا used for the believer is now used for the disbeliever, but in a completely different context. He used to be really happy when he went back to his family. He had no worries, no concerns.

The word أهل here can mean biological family, but it also means the people you fit in with, the people you belong with, your company. When people deserve one another or are qualified for something, they are أهل to each other. So this could be his family, but it could also be his gang, his cronies, the people he spent all his time with. He was completely content and comfortable among them.

translation: Indeed, he had thought he would never return [to Allah]
commentary:
إِنَّهُۥ ظَنَّ أَن لَّن يَحُورَ

He was convinced that he would never return. The word حور in Arabic means to return to the original state. تحولات are transformations, but حور specifically means returning to what you originally were.

He didn't think he would ever come back to being a human again. "Once I'm dead, I'm dead. Who's going to bring me back to life? When we are decayed bones and you can't even tell what body part they used to be, we're going to be created as a new creation? That's impossible."

The disbelievers asked sarcastically: "Is there only this one life? We die and we live, and nothing destroys us except time. Once time has killed us, there's no reversing it. We're not coming back." He was absolutely convinced he would never be resurrected. That's why he behaved the way he behaved.

Notice that this surah doesn't describe what his behavior actually was, because the previous surahs already established that. Now it simply states: he didn't think he'd ever come back.

The word حور also carries another meaning: hesitation or doubt. لن يحور can also mean he was convinced he would never even doubt or question whether resurrection might happen. That thought didn't even occur to him. He didn't allow that consideration to enter his mind at all.

translation: But yes! Indeed, his Lord was ever of him, Seeing
commentary:
بَلَىٰٓ إِنَّ رَبَّهُۥ كَانَ بِهِۦ بَصِيرًا

But yes! Indeed, his Lord was ever Seeing of him. The word بصير comes from بصر, which is different from نظر. Both mean "to see," but بصر means to keep your eye on something constantly, to maintain a permanent watch. It's like the word used for Maryam's (AS) sister: "And his sister watched from a distance" – maintaining continuous observation.

Allah ﷻ had always been keeping constant watch over him. There was never a moment when Allah ﷻ did not have him under complete observation. كان به بصيرًا – his Lord was always fully aware, constantly watching.

This is the culmination of the entire discussion. He thought he would never be brought back, yet Allah ﷻ was watching him all along. This brings to a close the second and largest section of the surah.

Notice how this section presented the journey's end without describing all the stages in between – childhood, aging, death, the grave. Other places in the Quran describe those stages. Here, because this surah is about finality, Allah ﷻ takes us directly to the ultimate destination: the book in the right hand or the book behind the back. May Allah ﷻ make us among those with the book in the right hand.

translation: So I swear by the twilight glow
commentary:
فَلَآ أُقْسِمُ بِٱلشَّفَقِ

I swear by the twilight. شفق is the orange-reddish glow of the sky about twenty minutes before Maghrib, after Asr time has passed. That light hitting the horizon tells you night is coming. There is no way to save that day anymore – it's almost Maghrib time. The sun is turning red and its light is being taken away. Darkness is about to settle in.

translation: And [by] the night and what it envelops
commentary:
وَٱلَّيْلِ وَمَا وَسَقَ

And by the night and what it gathers. وسق means to wrap something up, to gather things and hold them within a fold. جمع means to gather or collect, but وسق specifically means to envelope, to wrap around like a blanket.

Some commentators say the night envelopes the light – wrapping up the entire world like a blanket. Others say the night envelopes the stars inside itself. The image is of night as a blanket with stars tucked inside it. That's the visual: والليل وما وسق – the night and everything it gathers within its fold.

translation: And [by] the moon when it becomes full
commentary:
وَٱلْقَمَرِ إِذَا ٱتَّسَقَ

And by the moon when it becomes full. اتسق means to gather together, to become complete. The moon as it moves forward, gathering more and more light – the first day's moon, then the second, then the third – getting thicker and fuller as it gathers more light around itself until it becomes full.

These three oaths have something profound in common: they all describe processes. The day doesn't suddenly become night. It slowly, gradually transitions. You don't notice each second passing, but sometimes you look up and realize: "Oh, it's already almost Maghrib. I haven't even prayed Asr yet." Time passes in a process, and before you know it, you've reached the end point.

Then there's Maghrib, and eventually the blue sky turns completely black. والليل وما وسق – by the night and everything it wraps up. Before you know it, the time is gone. You don't even realize how quickly it happens.

Think about Ramadan. It starts and feels like it will last forever because we've only had one fast. But before you know it – two weeks have already passed. How did that happen so quickly?

والقمر إذا اتسق – things that follow a process creep up on you seemingly out of nowhere. You're sitting with your children and suddenly you look at them: "When did you get this tall? When did that happen?"

This notion of things moving gradually toward their conclusion – that's what these oaths establish.

translation: [That] you will surely experience state after state
commentary:
لَتَرْكَبُنَّ طَبَقًا عَن طَبَقٍ

You will surely ride stage after stage. This is the response to the oaths. Just as the day transitions through stages to night, just as the moon moves through phases to fullness, you are absolutely going to progress through one stage after another after another.

The Prophet ﷺ said this ayah was about himself. Early in Makkah, nobody would listen to what he was saying. Things were only getting worse and worse, like the night getting darker and darker. The light of Islam was like the slightest crescent moon. But eventually that moon would gain more and more momentum, and the light would shine brighter and brighter. Eventually even that moon wouldn't be enough – the entire night would be replaced by full daylight.

"Yes, you're going through struggles right now. There's persecution in Makkah right now. There will even be attempts to hurt and kill you. But eventually there will be Hijrah. Then there will be Badr. Then Uhud. Then Ahzab. Then Hudaybiyyah. Then the conquest of Makkah. It will take stages – it doesn't happen overnight. Each level, you have to cross through one after another."

The other meaning applies to all of us: whether you like it or not, you are going from one stage to the next to the next, and you have no say in it. You were inside your mother's womb – you loved it there, but you had to leave. When you were a baby being carried everywhere, eventually you had to walk. Then you had to talk. Then you were thrown into school whether you liked it or not. You grew older whether you liked it or not.

The hair starts changing colors – you can dye it all you want, but you know what's really happening. Some of that hair starts quitting its job, saying "I'm done, goodbye" – and you can do nothing about it. You can put in fake plugs if you want, but it's going. The skin starts getting wrinkly. You can do nothing about it. Eyesight weakens. Bones become more brittle. You don't move like you used to. You can't sit like you used to. You can't stand or pray like you used to. You won't be able to remember things. Your memory starts failing.

One stage after another after another. Your back will bend. You'll be looking down toward the ground where you're going to end up anyway. Then you'll be in that grave, and you can't do anything about it. Your body will be washed, my body will be washed, and it won't be in our control. We'll be inside that patch of dirt. Then Allah ﷻ will pull us out and we'll have no say. Stage after stage after stage after stage – you are headed toward Allah ﷻ.

This ayah echoes the earlier statement: يا أيها الإنسان إنك كادح إلى ربك كدحًا – the same concept explained again through these oaths.

translation: So what is [the matter] with them [that] they do not believe
commentary:
فَمَا لَهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ

Then what is wrong with them that they do not believe? If this progression through stages is so clear to them – they're getting older, the sun sets every day, the night comes, the moon goes through its phases – why don't they believe? They see it happening. This isn't hidden evidence. They witness it constantly. Yet still they don't believe.

translation: And when the Qur'an is recited to them, they do not prostrate [to Allah]
commentary:
وَإِذَا قُرِئَ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْقُرْءَانُ لَا يَسْجُدُونَ ۩

And when the Quran is recited to them, they do not prostrate. This is a place where there is scholarly difference of opinion about whether we should make sajdah, but we are on the side of caution and perform it.

When the Quran is recited to them, they don't fall into prostration. They don't react. Let me remind you of something from the beginning of this surah: the skies were desperately waiting for Allah's ﷻ word, ready to tear open. The earth was desperately waiting for Allah's ﷻ word so it could stretch and throw out everything inside it. Everything else in creation is desperate to respond to Allah's ﷻ word.

Here you have human beings listening to the word of Allah ﷻ, and they don't even budge. The Quran is recited to them and they don't fall into sajdah. The contrast is stark. Everything else desperately wants to hear and respond to Allah's ﷻ word, but these humans remain unmoved.

لا يسجدون doesn't only mean the physical act of putting your head on the ground. It means submitting yourself to Allah's ﷻ instructions, even when they go against your greed, your desires, your worldly ambitions.

translation: But those who have disbelieved deny
commentary:
بَلِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يُكَذِّبُونَ

Rather, those who disbelieve deny. The word يكذبون is intensive – they want to call all of it a lie. Not يكذبون which would mean simply "they lie," but يكذبون – they are determined to call everything a lie. The Prophet ﷺ is a liar. The Quran is a lie. The promise of resurrection is a lie. All of it – lies.

Why do they want to call it all lies? Because these teachings are getting in their way. People want to do certain things, earn certain things, profit in certain ways. When the teachings of our religion get in the way of those pursuits, the easy solution is: "I don't want to deal with this. It's all lies."

translation: And Allah is most knowing of what they keep within themselves
commentary:
وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يُوعُونَ

And Allah is most knowing of what they hoard. يوعون comes from وعاء – a container or bag. In the verb form أوعى, it means to hoard something, to keep amassing and storing away in containers. This builds on the theme from the previous surah about people who hoard wealth even by cheating their customers a little.

When people become greedy, when they just want to earn and earn and earn, and then criticism comes from Allah ﷻ, they don't want to submit. لا يسجدون expands in meaning – it's not just about putting your head on the ground. It's about submitting to Allah's ﷻ instructions even when they conflict with your greed.

Let me take a moment to help you understand this point, because it's crucial. The people of Makkah didn't just worship idols. They worshipped money. Each idol was a representative of a different tribe. Each tribe would come to Makkah to pay respect to their idol. This created a religious tourism industry. That entire tribe would come, do their business, buy and sell, stay in hotels – the whole hospitality industry.

All of Makkah's wealth came because the idols were there. No tribe would attack Makkah because their idol was there. The idols provided political security and economic security. When they refused to remove the idols, it was because removing them would destroy their income, their entire economic system.

يوعون – they were bagging, hoarding, profiting. Allah ﷻ knows what they're accumulating. يوعون can mean spiritually – Allah ﷻ knows better what deeds they're gathering. They think they're doing good, but they're actually doing terrible things: يَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ يُحْسِنُونَ صُنْعًا – "thinking they are doing good" (18:104). Allah ﷻ knows better than they do what they're truly earning.

But there's also a sharp political and economic commentary here on the corruption of the Quraysh leadership. أوعى also means to hide and keep away secretly – they're doing all of this but showing a different picture. Allah ﷻ will later expose their false religiosity: "Woe to those who pray, who are heedless of their prayer, those who make show [of their deeds] and withhold [simple] assistance" (107:4-7). They pray, but only for show. The reality is they don't care about a single orphan or needy person. Yet they're the custodians of religion. That's their reality.

translation: So give them tidings of a painful punishment
commentary:
فَبَشِّرْهُم بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ

So give them tidings of a painful punishment. بشّر typically means to give good news, glad tidings. Here it's used sarcastically: "Congratulate them." Give them the good news that a painful punishment is coming their way.

This is a devastating irony – using the word for good news to announce their doom.

translation: Except for those who believe and do righteous deeds. For them is a reward uninterrupted
commentary:
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَهُمْ أَجْرٌ غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍۭ

Except those who believe and do righteous deeds – for them is a reward uninterrupted. This exception can be understood two ways grammatically:

First, as استثناء متصل – a connected exception: "Congratulate them all with painful punishment, except those among them who decide to repent, convert, believe and do good deeds. They will be rewarded." This highlights that the door of repentance is always open.

Second, as استثناء منفصل – a disconnected exception: "All of them will be punished except those who have already believed" – meaning the Muslims are being highlighted separately. All will face punishment except the believers.

Then comes my favorite word in this entire surah: غير ممنون. The word منّ in Arabic has two meanings.

First, منّ from قطع – to cut. قطع المنّة means to cut something off, to discontinue it. غير ممنون means the reward Allah ﷻ will give them will never be discontinued. It will keep going and growing. Your iman, your good deeds, your salah, the good you do, the truth you speak – all of it will continue growing in your scales. The reward will never be taken away. Nobody can steal it from you. It's endless, uninterrupted, never cut off.

Second, منّ also means to remind someone that you did them a favor. Allah ﷻ warns us: لَا تُبْطِلُوا صَدَقَاتِكُم بِالْمَنِّ – "Do not invalidate your charities by reminders [of it]" (2:264). When you give someone something and then keep reminding them – "Enjoying that present I gave you? It was pretty expensive, you know" – you make them feel degraded.

غير ممنون means nobody will ever make you feel that this reward is a grudging favor. You'll be honored in the way you're rewarded. It will be dignified. Only Allah ﷻ will remind you that this is His favor, but He does so in a way that honors you, not degrades you.

This brings us to the close of this beautiful, powerful surah – a surah about finality, about the inevitable journey we're all on toward Allah ﷻ, and about the two ultimate destinations that await us. May Allah ﷻ make us among those who receive their book in the right hand, who have an easy reckoning, who return to their families in joy, and who receive a reward that is uninterrupted and given with honor.
